title: What Is Dollar Cost Averaging? The Beginner's Guide
desc: Dollar cost averaging explained — what it is, why it works, and how to automate it for stocks, ETFs, and crypto.

sections.body:
Dollar cost averaging (DCA) means investing a fixed dollar amount on a regular schedule — weekly, biweekly, or monthly — regardless of what the market is doing.

When prices are low, your fixed amount buys more shares. When prices are high, it buys fewer. Over time, this smooths out your average purchase cost.

DCA works for two reasons:

**1. Removes emotion.** The biggest investing mistake is panic-selling during crashes. DCA forces you to keep buying even when everything looks scary — exactly when the best deals are available.

**2. Removes the need to time the market.** Even professional fund managers can't reliably time the market. DCA sidesteps the problem entirely.

Crypto DCA example: $100/week into Bitcoin starting January 2022 (near the peak). By 2025, that investor had a significant profit despite starting at what felt like the worst possible time.

Consistency beats timing. Use Coinbase to set up automatic weekly crypto buys and stop agonizing over price.

title: How to Open a Roth IRA in 2026 (Step-by-Step)
desc: A complete beginner's guide to Roth IRAs — what they are, why they're powerful, 2026 contribution limits, and how to open one in minutes.

sections.body:
IRA = Individual Retirement Account. The 'Roth' version has a unique tax structure: pay taxes now, never pay taxes again on the growth.

For most people under 40, the Roth IRA is the single best investment account available — better than a regular brokerage account because of tax-free compounding.

In 2026, you can contribute up to **$7,000/year** to a Roth IRA ($8,000 if you're 50+).

Income limits: full contribution allowed under $146,000 (single) or $230,000 (married filing jointly).

$7,000/year = ~$583/month = ~$134/week.

The barrier is lower than most people think:
• No minimum balance at most brokers
• 10–15 minutes to set up
• Start investing the same day

The most common mistake: waiting until you have 'enough.' Start with $50. Time is the main ingredient — and every year you wait costs you thousands in compounding.

title: Index Funds: The Investment Even Warren Buffett Recommends
desc: Index funds explained from scratch — what they are, why they outperform most active investors, and how to buy your first one today.

sections.body:
An index fund holds stocks that mirror a market index — most commonly the S&P 500 (500 largest US companies).

Instead of picking individual stocks, you buy tiny pieces of all 500 companies at once. When the market goes up, your fund goes up.

The S&P 500 has returned an average of ~10.5% annually since 1957.

sections.h: Why They Beat Most Active Investors
sections.body:
Over any 15-year period, more than **90% of actively managed mutual funds underperform the S&P 500 index**.

Professional fund managers — with MBAs, Bloomberg terminals, and decades of experience — can't beat a simple index fund 9 out of 10 times. Yet they charge 1–2% in annual fees.

sections.h: The Best Index Funds to Start With
sections.body:
Start simple:
• **US exposure:** VOO or VTI
• **Global exposure:** VT, or 60% VTI + 40% VXUS
• **One fund forever:** VT

Don't overthink it. Pick one, automate monthly purchases, and check prices less often. That's the entire strategy.

title: Lightning Network Explained: Why Bitcoin Payments Are Now Instant
desc: The Bitcoin Lightning Network explained simply — what it is, how it makes Bitcoin instant and free, and why Strike is built on it.

sections.body:
Bitcoin is great as a store of value but has a limitation: the main blockchain processes only ~7 transactions per second. Visa handles 24,000 per second.

Bitcoin transactions can take 10+ minutes and cost $5–$50 in fees during busy periods — terrible for everyday payments. The Lightning Network solves this.

Lightning creates 'payment channels' between users — like running a tab at a bar, settling multiple transactions at once.

Inside a Lightning channel: transactions are **instant and nearly free**. Only opening and closing a channel touches the main blockchain.

Result: thousands of transactions per second, fees under $0.01, settlement in milliseconds.

Strike abstracts away all Lightning Network complexity into a clean, simple app. You don't need to understand payment channels — it just works.

For buying Bitcoin, Strike is one of the lowest-fee options available. The first $500 is completely free, and fees after that remain very competitive.

title: Earn Crypto on Every Purchase: Gemini Credit Card Full Review
desc: Full review of the Gemini Credit Card — 4% dining rewards, how real-time crypto cashback works, who it's best for, and whether it's worth applying.

sections.h: What Is the Gemini Credit Card?
sections.body:
A Mastercard that converts everyday spending into cryptocurrency. You choose which coin receives your rewards — Bitcoin, Ethereum, or any of 60+ assets.

Key facts: No annual fee. No foreign transaction fees. Rewards hit your account in seconds, not at month-end.

Every time you swipe, your cashback converts to crypto at the current market rate and deposits into your Gemini account within seconds. No waiting, no minimums, no manual redemption.

This means your dining cashback starts earning price appreciation immediately. If you earn 4% back in Bitcoin and Bitcoin goes up, your rewards are worth more.

To put 4% in context: the Capital One Savor offers 4% dining cashback — for a $95 annual fee. Gemini matches that rate with zero annual fee, with the caveat that rewards are in crypto.

If you're comfortable holding some crypto, this is one of the best dining reward rates available anywhere.
